title: What are the steps to reset Firefox browser to fix performance issues?
content: To reset Mozilla Firefox and resolve performance issues, open the browser and click the menu icon (three horizontal lines) in the top-right corner, then select Help and choose More Troubleshooting Information. On the page that opens, click Refresh Firefox, and confirm the action in the pop-up window. The browser will automatically close and restore itself to default settings while keeping important personal data such as bookmarks, saved passwords, and browsing history. This process removes extensions, themes, and customized settings that may be causing slow speed or instability in Reset Firefox Browser.

title: FireFox for Windows auto update - proxy issue
content:
Problem Description

In a server-based environment, I need to implement automatic updates for Mozilla Firefox, while maintaining strict outbound network control.

The architectural requirement is as follows: • Only the update component should be allowed to access the Internet via a proxy. • The browser itself (user browsing sessions) must not inherit or use that proxy configuration. • The proxy configuration for the update mechanism should be logically separated from the browser’s network configuration.

⸻

The Issue

In contrast to the update mechanism used by Google Chrome (Google Update / Omaha), where: • The updater runs as a separate system service • It supports dedicated proxy configuration • It does not inherit browser-level proxy settings • It allows granular control of outbound traffic exclusively for update operations

In Mozilla Firefox: • The update mechanism is tightly integrated with the browser • There is no native, dedicated proxy configuration exclusively for the updater • Proxy configuration applies to the entire browser • It is difficult to implement an “update-only network access” model

title: Did Not Connect: Potential Security Issue, Firefox detected a potential security threat and did not continue to google.com because this website requires a secure connection.
content:
Did Not Connect: Potential Security Issue

Firefox detected a potential security threat and did not continue to google.com because this website requires a secure connection.

What can you do about it?

google.com has a security policy called HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS), which means that Firefox can only connect to it securely. You can’t add an exception to visit this site.

The issue is most likely with the website, and there is nothing you can do to resolve it. You can notify the website’s administrator about the problem.

title: extem
content:
Marking support threads as "Solved"

The Original Poster (OP), also called the "Question owner", can select Solves the problem next to an answer, to mark the thread they started as "Solved". Moderators can also mark threads as solved when the Original Poster (OP) clearly indicates the problem is solved (such as "thank you, problem fixed now" or "ok, it looks like it worked" etc.). We will not mark as solved threads which appear to be solved and probably are but where the OP did not make any comment. Multiple Accounts

In certain cases, multiple accounts are acceptable for use on Mozilla Support, for example: 

   Moderators or Reviewers who want an account to view or participate in the Mozilla Support site without special permissions.    SUMO members with a recognized role, such as Moderator, who want anonymity to ask a question, for personal privacy.    Users who are having trouble signing in to their account might create a new one.

It's recommended that you indicate any alternate profiles in your primary user profile.

Multiple accounts cannot be used for trolling, spamming, phishing for private information, gaming the helpful votes in the forum, or for any other behavior that violates our rules and guidelines. See also Consequences of Unacceptable Behavior. bold text

title: Instalação duplicada do Firefox 64-bits (uma em Program Files e outra em Program Files (x86)
content:
Prezados,

Hoje ocorreu uma situação incomum no meu computador com Windows.

Eu já utilizava o Firefox atualizado na versão **147.0.4 (64-bits)**. Durante o uso normal do navegador, aparentemente foi iniciada outra instalação do Firefox, mesmo a versão já estando atualizada.

Ao verificar no **Painel de Controle > Programas e Recursos**, identifiquei que agora constam **duas instalações do Mozilla Firefox 64-bits**:

  • Uma instalada em: 
 `C:\Program Files\Mozilla Firefox`
  • Outra instalada em: 
 `C:\Program Files (x86)\Mozilla Firefox`

Ambas constam como versão 64-bits.

Minhas dúvidas são:

1. É normal existir uma instalação 64-bits dentro da pasta *Program Files (x86)*? 2. Pode ter ocorrido algum tipo de atualização incorreta ou conflito? 3. Qual seria a forma correta e segura de remover a instalação duplicada? 4. Há risco de impacto em perfil, favoritos ou extensões?

Observação: Não alterei manualmente diretórios nem executei instalador externo recentemente — a situação aparentemente ocorreu de forma automática.

Agradeço desde já qualquer orientação técnica.

All bookmarks came over, everything looks great, except that none of my open tabs from the desktop browser show on my laptop.

I would like to have identical tabs open across devices. Is there a way to configure this where a new computer being logged in automatically receives all open tabs from the initial device and then tabs automatically sync based on whatever is opened/closed on either device after the initial tabs are identically synced?
